enonce.texte:
Exercise 1: Read the sentences below. Identify and underline the determiner or quantifier in each sentence. Then, classify it as an article (a/an/the), a quantifier (some, any, no, much, many, a little, a few, both, either, neither), or a demonstrative (this/that/these/those - if applicable, though not the main focus of the lesson, it's useful to mention).

Example: I need some water. -> quantifier

questions.texte: She adopted a kitten from the shelter.
I don't have any money left.
Neither option seems good to me.

intitule: Fill in the Blanks with Appropriate Quantifiers

enonce.texte: Exercise 2: Complete each sentence with a suitable quantifier from the list: some, any, no, much, many, a little, a few, both, either, neither. Some sentences may have more than one correct answer, but choose the most logical one.

questions.texte: There are only _______ apples left in the basket.
She doesn't have _______ patience for slow drivers.
_______ of my parents is a teacher; my mother is a nurse and my father is an engineer.
I have _______ idea what you are talking about.

intitule: Choose the Correct Determiner (A/An/The or Zero Article)

enonce.texte: Exercise 3: Choose the correct article (a, an, the) or leave blank (Ø) for zero article.

questions.texte: She went to _______ hospital to visit her friend. (general purpose)

intitule: Real-Life Application: Writing a Shopping List

enonce.texte: Exercise 4: You are planning a small party for 10 friends. Write a shopping list of 8 items. For each item, use a different determiner or quantifier (e.g., a bottle of, some, a few, both, no, much, many, a little, either, neither). Then, write 3 complete sentences explaining your choices.
images: A simple party scene with balloons, snacks, and drinks, with a blank shopping list next to it.

questions.texte: Write your shopping list (8 items with determiners/quantifiers).
questions.reponse: Example: 1. A cake, 2. Some soda, 3. A few bags of chips, 4. Both chocolate and vanilla ice cream, 5. No peanuts (allergy), 6. Much lemonade, 7. A little salt, 8. Neither spicy nor sour candy.

sousQuestions.texte: Write 3 sentences explaining why you chose specific quantifiers (e.g., 'I bought a little salt because too much is unhealthy.').
sousQuestions.reponse: Example: 'I bought a few bags of chips because 10 people will eat them. I chose no peanuts because one friend has an allergy. I got both types of ice cream to satisfy different tastes.'

enonce.texte:
Exercise 5: The following paragraph contains 8 errors in the use of determiners and quantifiers (articles, some, any, much, many, a little, a few, both, either, neither). Identify and correct each error. Rewrite the corrected paragraph.

Paragraph: 'I went to a market yesterday to buy few vegetables. I needed much tomatoes for a sauce, but there wasn't some fresh ones. The seller had both red or green tomatoes. I don't like neither type very much, so I bought a little tomatoes. Then I wanted some bread, but I had no money left. I had a little change in my pocket, just enough for a bread. Finally, I bought an bread and went home. There wasn't many happiness in my shopping trip.'

questions.reponse: 1. 'few' → 'a few' (positive sense) OR 'some' (better). 2. 'much' → 'many' (tomatoes countable). 3. 'some' → 'any' (negative sentence). 4. 'both...or' → 'both...and'. 5. 'don't like neither' → 'like neither' OR 'don't like either'. 6. 'a little tomatoes' → 'a few tomatoes' (countable). 7. 'a bread' → 'some bread' (uncountable) OR 'a loaf of bread'. 8. 'an bread' → 'a bread' or better 'some bread'.

sousQuestions.reponse: Corrected paragraph: 'I went to a market yesterday to buy a few vegetables. I needed many tomatoes for a sauce, but there weren't any fresh ones. The seller had both red and green tomatoes. I like neither type very much, so I bought a few tomatoes. Then I wanted some bread, but I had no money left. I had a little change in my pocket, just enough for some bread. Finally, I bought some bread and went home. There wasn't much happiness in my shopping trip.'

enonce.texte: Exercise 6: Imagine you are a customer at a grocery store and the seller is your classmate. Write a short dialogue (8-10 exchanges) where you use at least 8 different determiners/quantifiers from the lesson: a, an, the, some, any, no, much, many, a little, a few, both, either, neither. Underline each determiner/quantifier you use. The situation: You are buying ingredients for a traditional Cameroonian dish (e.g., Eru, Ndolé, or Plantain with fish).
images: A market scene in Cameroon with sellers and buyers, baskets of plantains, fish, and leafy vegetables.

questions.texte: Write the dialogue (8-10 exchanges). Underline each of the 8+ determiners/quantifiers.
questions.reponse: Example dialogue: Customer: 'Good morning! Do you have <u>any</u> fresh fish today?' Seller: 'Yes, I have <u>both</u> smoked and fresh fish.' Customer: 'I'll take <u>a</u> kilogram of fresh fish. How <u>much</u> is it?' Seller: '2000 francs. Would you like <u>some</u> plantains as well?' Customer: 'Yes, but <u>no</u> green ones. I need ripe plantains.' Seller: 'I have <u>a few</u> ripe ones left.' Customer: 'Great. I'll take <u>both</u> of those. Is there <u>any</u> eru leaves?' Seller: 'Sorry, <u>neither</u> eru nor ndolé is available today.' Customer: 'That's fine. I'll take <u>a little</u> palm oil then.'
